---
title: "lab4"
author: "Eleanore Brugh"
date: "2026-04-13"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.



```{r packages, echo=TRUE, message=TRUE}

library(tidyverse)
library(terra)
library(sf)
library(tmap)
library(fs)
```

```{r load data}

data_dir <- "erie_raster_ex"
images_dir <- file.path(data_dir, "images")
bounds_dir <- file.path(data_dir, "bounds")

```


#Task 1 Data Management and Reprocessing 
```{r list_files, echo=TRUE, message=TRUE}

raster_files <- fs::dir_ls(images_dir, regexp = "\\.tif$")

raster_files
length(raster_files)

#7

```

#Task 1.2
```{r}

r <- terra::rast(raster_files[1])

terra::crs(r) #WGS 84 UTM zone 17N
terra::ext(r) #xmin= 281282.934996, xmax= 694382.934996, ymin= 4572608.997997, ymax= 4764308.997997 
terra::res(r) #300x300 
terra::nlyr(r) #1
terra::global(r, c("min", "max"), na.rm = TRUE) #min= 1, max= 252

```



#Task 1.3
```{r}

clean_cicyano <- function(r){
  rcl <- matrix(
    c(
      0, 0, NA,
      250, 255, NA
    ),
    ncol = 3,
    byrow = TRUE)
  
  cleaned_r <- terra::classify(r, rcl, right = NA, others = NULL)
  
  return(cleaned_r)
}
  
#test
r_clean <- clean_cicyano(r)

terra::global(r, c("min", "max"), na.rm = TRUE)
terra::global(r_clean, c("min", "max"), na.rm = TRUE) #min = 1, max = 249

```

#Task 1.4
```{r}

dn_to_ci <- function(r){
  ci_r <- 10^(((3/250) * r) - 4.2)
  return(ci_r)
}

r_ci <- dn_to_ci(r_clean)

terra::global(r_ci, c("min", "max"), na.rm = TRUE)

```

#T1 Questions
1.1 The CRS is WGS84 UTM Zone17. It's projected with linear units, so converting to square kilometers would not require a re-projection. 

1.2 Sentinel values like the clouds are not real data and have to be removed so that they don't get converted to look like real algal bloom data.

1.3 Yes it kept the lower boundary of 0 and upper boundary of 250 in the new classification so that the sentinel values could be accurately re-classified as NA.

#Task 2
```{r load}

sandusky <- sf::read_sf(file.path(bounds_dir, "bb_erie_sandusky.shp"))

sf::st_crs(sandusky)

sandusky <- sf::st_transform(sandusky, 32617)

```

#Task 2.2
```{r}

aug_16 <- terra::rast(file.path(images_dir, "ts_2017.0816_0822.L4.LE3.CIcyano.MAXIMUM_7day.tif"))

aug_16_clean <- clean_cicyano(aug_16)
aug_16_ci <- dn_to_ci(aug_16_clean)

sandusky_crop <- terra::crop(aug_16_ci, sandusky)
sandusky_0816 <- terra::mask(sandusky_crop, sandusky)

```

#Task 2.3
```{r}

terra::global(aug_16_ci, "mean", na.rm = TRUE) #0.0004572394
terra::global(sandusky_0816, "mean", na.rm = TRUE) #0.0171323

```

#Task 2.4
```{r map}

tm_shape(sandusky_0816) +
  tm_raster(
    title = "CI Value") +
  tm_layout(
    title = "Cyanobacteria in Sandusky Bay \nAugust 16-22, 2017") +
  tm_scalebar()

```

#Questions Task 2
2.1 terra::crop reduces the data size and crops the raster into a box. terra_mask trims the shape to match the actual shape of sandusk bay and remove everything outside of that boundary. using only crop would leave excess data. 

2.2 The bay differs from the full lake because the bay has increased input from rivers, it is shallower, and has a higher temperature (all of these contribute to cyanobacteria production) which can cause differing in cyanobacteria concentrations throughout the lake. It doesn't mean there is more or less concentration, it just shows the variablility throughtout the lake. 


#Task 3
```{r}

selected_files <- sort(raster_files)[c(1, 3, 5, 7)]

raster_list <- lapply(selected_files, function(f) {
  r <- terra::rast(f)
  r_clean <- clean_cicyano(r)
  r_ci <- dn_to_ci(r_clean)
  return(r_ci)
})

r_stack <- terra::rast(raster_list)

r_stack

```

#Task 3.2
```{r}

mean_values <- terra::global(r_stack, "mean", na.rm = TRUE)

mean_df <- data.frame(
  date = stringr::str_extract(basename(selected_files), "\\d{4}_\\d{4}"), 
  mean_ci = mean_values$mean 
)

mean_df

```

#Task 3.3
```{r}


ggplot(mean_df, aes(x = date, y = mean_ci, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean CI Values Over Time",
    subtitle = "Sandusky Bay",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Mean CI Value"
  ) 
#0823_0829
  
```

#Task 3.4
```{r}

early_r <- r_stack[[1]]
late_r <- r_stack[[4]]

early_bin <- !is.na(early_r)
late_bin <- !is.na(late_r)

persistent_bloom <- early_bin & late_bin #both dates

new_bloom <- (!early_bin) & late_bin


tm_shape(persistent_bloom) +
  tm_raster(col.legend = tm_legend(title = "Persistent Bloom")) +
  tm_scalebar()

tm_shape(new_bloom) +
  tm_raster(col.legend = tm_legend(title = "New Bloom")) +
  tm_scalebar

```

#Task 3 Questions
3.1 The mean CI value is the average of the entire lake so it can hide spatial patterns. Small areas with high concentrations of blooms could have little representation in the data due to large areas with low blooms. A better alternative might be measuring an area in the lake that has more blooms. 

3.2 The rasters must all be aligned on the same grid, if the layers are offset by different grids then the pixels will be overlapping and distort the stack. 

#Task 4
```{r}

aoi_files <- fs::dir_ls(bounds_dir, regexp = "\\.shp$")

aoi_list <- lapply(aoi_files, function(f) {
  x <- sf::read_sf(f)
  x$aoi_name <- basename(f)
  x
})

all_aois <- dplyr::bind_rows(aoi_list)

all_aois

```

#Task 4.1
```{r}

peak_raster <- r_stack[[2]]

extract_df <- terra::extract(peak_raster, all_aois)


```
